Reclamation projects bring irrigation to the Rio Grande Valley
1900s.economic_conditions: The economy diversified modestly. Tourism began as the railroad brought Eastern visitors to experience Pueblo cultures, the desert landscape, and the territory's exotic (to them) blend of Spanish, indigenous, and frontier cultures. The Fred Harvey Company promoted the Southwest as a tourist destination. Mining, ranching, and agriculture continued as the economic base. The art colony at Taos signaled a new economic force — the commodification of New Mexico's cultural heritage.
1900s.migration_patterns: Artists from the East discovered New Mexico's extraordinary light, landscape, and cultures. Bert Phillips and Ernest Blumenschein arrived in Taos (1898) and began the art colony that would make Taos and Santa Fe famous. Anglo-American settlement continued. Hispanic communities maintained their villages but with a reduced land base.
1900s.social_context: The arrival of artists introduced a new dynamic — outsiders who romanticized New Mexico's indigenous and hispano cultures while simultaneously changing them. The Fred Harvey Company marketed Pueblo peoples as tourist attractions. The territory's three cultures — Anglo, hispano, and indigenous — coexisted in a complex hierarchy that tourists barely understood.
1900s.daily_life_snapshot: At Taos Pueblo, tourists arrived by railroad and stagecoach to photograph the multi-story adobe buildings and buy pottery. In Santa Fe, artists set up easels in the plaza. In the hispano villages, life continued as before — acequias, sheep, church, fiestas — but the world was closing in. At the mines, workers extracted wealth from the earth while the territory waited for statehood.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Statehood still denied
Land grant losses continue impoverishing hispano communities
Commodification of indigenous cultures raises ethical concerns
Drought and economic fluctuations
narrative_hooks: An Eastern artist arrives in Taos and sees the Pueblo for the first time — the golden adobe against blue sky — and knows he will never leave
A Pueblo woman sells pottery to tourists at the railroad station, translating centuries of artistic tradition into cash for her family

key_events: New Mexico achieves statehood (January 6, 1912) — the 47th state
Mexican Revolution sends refugees across the border
Pancho Villa raids Columbus, New Mexico (March 9, 1916) — U.S. sends Punitive Expedition into Mexico
World War I — New Mexico contributes soldiers, including many Hispanic and Pueblo men
Taos art colony grows; Mabel Dodge Luhan arrives (1917)
1910s.economic_conditions: Statehood brought increased federal investment and political representation. The Mexican Revolution created border instability but also drove economic activity — the Punitive Expedition into Mexico employed thousands. World War I boosted demand for New Mexico's agricultural and mineral products. The art colony at Taos and Santa Fe began generating tourism revenue. The economy remained largely rural and resource-based.
1910s.migration_patterns: Statehood attracted new settlers. The Mexican Revolution drove refugees into southern New Mexico. The Punitive Expedition brought military personnel. Artists continued arriving in Taos and Santa Fe. Hispanic and Pueblo men served in World War I, gaining exposure to the wider world.
1910s.social_context: Villa's raid on Columbus was the last foreign military attack on the continental United States. The Punitive Expedition — 10,000 troops under General Pershing — failed to capture Villa but demonstrated American military power. Statehood finally gave New Mexico political voice but did not resolve the land grant injustices. World War I service by Hispanic and Pueblo soldiers created expectations of equal citizenship that would fuel later civil rights efforts.
1910s.daily_life_snapshot: On the morning of March 9, 1916, Pancho Villa's raiders crossed the border and attacked Columbus, New Mexico, killing 18 Americans. The U.S. response — the Punitive Expedition — turned the border into a military zone. In Taos, Mabel Dodge Luhan arrived from New York and began creating the salon that would attract D.H. Lawrence, Georgia O'Keeffe, and other luminaries. In the villages, Gold Star families mourned sons lost in France.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Pancho Villa raid on Columbus (March 9, 1916) — 18 Americans killed
Influenza pandemic (1918-1919) devastates New Mexico communities, especially Pueblo and hispano villages
War casualties among Hispanic and Pueblo soldiers
Border instability from Mexican Revolution
narrative_hooks: A soldier in Columbus, New Mexico, wakes to gunfire at 4 AM on March 9, 1916, as Pancho Villa's raiders attack the sleeping town
Mabel Dodge Luhan arrives in Taos from Greenwich Village and falls in love with a Pueblo man, a landscape, and a way of life that will define the rest of her days
A Pueblo soldier returns from France with a Bronze Star and the expectation that his service has earned him equal citizenship — he will wait decades for that promise

key_events: Bursum Bill (1922) threatens Pueblo land rights — defeated after national protest campaign
Taos and Santa Fe art colonies flourish — Georgia O'Keeffe, D.H. Lawrence, Willa Cather visit
All Pueblo Council reorganized to fight Bursum Bill — first pan-Pueblo political action in centuries
Highway construction opens New Mexico to automobile tourism
Oil and gas discovery in southeastern New Mexico (Lea County, late 1920s)
1920s.economic_conditions: The defeat of the Bursum Bill — which would have transferred Pueblo water and land rights to Anglo squatters — was a landmark in indigenous rights. The art colonies generated national attention and tourism revenue. Oil and gas discovery in the Permian Basin of southeastern New Mexico would eventually transform the state's economy. Ranching and agriculture continued as the economic base. Highway construction began replacing the railroad as the primary transportation system.
1920s.migration_patterns: Artists and writers continued arriving in Taos and Santa Fe. The art colony attracted wealthy Eastern patrons and tourists. Oil exploration brought workers to southeastern New Mexico. Internal migration from villages to towns continued.
1920s.social_context: The Bursum Bill fight was a turning point for indigenous rights in New Mexico. The All Pueblo Council — reviving the organizational spirit of the 1680 Pueblo Revolt — united Pueblo communities in political action. National supporters including artists, writers, and reformers joined the cause. The defeat of the bill protected Pueblo lands and demonstrated that political organizing could succeed.
1920s.daily_life_snapshot: In Santa Fe, Georgia O'Keeffe painted the landscapes and sky that would make her famous. At the All Pueblo Council, leaders from every pueblo gathered to oppose the Bursum Bill, standing together as their ancestors had in 1680. On Route 66, which crossed New Mexico, tourist cabins and gas stations appeared. In Lea County, wildcatters drilled into the Permian Basin, striking oil.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Bursum Bill threatens Pueblo land and water rights (defeated 1923)
Hispanic communities continue losing lands to Anglo interests
Drought affects agricultural production
Rural poverty persists in hispano and Pueblo communities
narrative_hooks: A Pueblo governor stands before Congress to testify against the Bursum Bill, speaking for a people who have defended this land for a thousand years
Georgia O'Keeffe paints a New Mexico sunset and realizes she has found the landscape that will define her art — and American modernism
A wildcatter in Lea County watches oil gush from a drill pipe and knows that southeastern New Mexico will never be the same

key_events: Great Depression hits New Mexico's rural economy hard
New Deal programs — CCC, WPA, Federal Art Project — provide employment and document New Mexico's culture
Dust Bowl conditions affect eastern New Mexico
Indian Reorganization Act (1934) strengthens tribal self-governance
Route 66 brings auto tourists through New Mexico
1930s.economic_conditions: The Depression devastated New Mexico's already impoverished rural communities. Hispano villages that had lost their land base had no reserves. Ranching and mining suffered from collapsed commodity prices. New Deal programs were critical — CCC camps employed young men, WPA built infrastructure, and the Federal Art Project documented New Mexico's cultural heritage. Route 66 brought tourist dollars.
1930s.migration_patterns: Some New Mexicans left for California during the Depression. Dust Bowl refugees from Texas and Oklahoma passed through on Route 66. Internal migration from villages to towns continued. New Deal programs brought workers to conservation and construction projects across the state.
1930s.social_context: The Depression was catastrophic for communities already impoverished by land grant losses. The New Deal provided economic relief and, through the Federal Art Project and Federal Writers' Project, the first systematic documentation of New Mexico's hispano and Pueblo cultures. The Indian Reorganization Act strengthened Pueblo self-governance. Route 66 culture — motels, diners, gas stations — created a new roadside landscape.
1930s.daily_life_snapshot: On Route 66, tourists in Model A Fords stopped at roadside trading posts to buy Navajo jewelry and Pueblo pottery. CCC boys built trails and lodges in the national forests. WPA artists painted murals in courthouses and post offices, depicting New Mexico's multicultural history. In the hispano villages, families survived on gardens, sheep, and federal relief.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Depression devastates already impoverished rural communities
Dust Bowl conditions damage eastern New Mexico agriculture
Ongoing poverty in hispano and Pueblo communities
Mining and ranching employment collapses
narrative_hooks: A WPA artist paints a mural in a Santa Fe post office, depicting Pueblo, hispano, and Anglo peoples sharing the landscape — an idealized vision of what New Mexico could be
An hispano farmer who lost his land twenty years ago now works for the CCC, planting trees on the national forest land that was once his village's common grazing ground

key_events: Manhattan Project: Los Alamos laboratory established in secret (1943) — the birthplace of the atomic bomb
Trinity Test: first nuclear weapon detonated at Trinity Site near Alamogordo (July 16, 1945)
World War II military installations across New Mexico — Kirtland, Holloman, White Sands
Navajo Code Talkers serve in the Pacific theater
Bataan Death March: New Mexico's 200th Coast Artillery (largely Hispanic) captured at Bataan (1942)
1940s.economic_conditions: World War II transformed New Mexico from a rural backwater into a center of military and scientific activity. Los Alamos — built in secret on a remote mesa — employed thousands of the world's top scientists. Kirtland Air Force Base, Holloman, White Sands Proving Ground, and other installations brought massive federal spending. The state's economy shifted permanently toward defense and government employment. The Bataan survivors' story — New Mexico suffered a higher per capita casualty rate than any other state — was the territory's most painful contribution to the war.
dominant_industries: nuclear weapons research and production (Los Alamos)
1940s.migration_patterns: Scientists and military personnel flooded into New Mexico. Los Alamos became a secret city of physicists, engineers, and their families. Military bases brought servicemembers from across the country. After the war, many stayed. Navajo Code Talkers returned to the reservation. Hispanic veterans returned expecting the full citizenship their service warranted.
1940s.social_context: The Manhattan Project made New Mexico the birthplace of the Atomic Age — the Trinity Test on July 16, 1945, changed human history. The project's secrecy created a strange parallel reality — the world's most advanced science conducted in a remote location where hispano villagers and Pueblo communities had no idea what was happening on the mesa above them. The Bataan Death March — in which New Mexico's 200th Coast Artillery (heavily Hispanic) suffered terribly — was the state's greatest wartime sacrifice.
1940s.daily_life_snapshot: At Los Alamos, Oppenheimer, Fermi, and other physicists worked in crude laboratory buildings on the mesa, their families living in hastily built housing. At Trinity Site near Alamogordo, the predawn darkness was shattered by a light brighter than a thousand suns — the world's first nuclear explosion. On Iwo Jima, Navajo Code Talkers transmitted messages in their ancient language. In the Bataan prisoner of war camps, New Mexico guardsmen endured unspeakable cruelty.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Bataan Death March (April 1942) — New Mexico's 200th Coast Artillery captured; hundreds die in the march and as POWs
Trinity Test radiation exposes nearby communities ('Tularosa downwinders')
Los Alamos construction displaces hispano homesteaders from the Pajarito Plateau
Navajo Code Talkers' service goes unrecognized for decades (classified)
narrative_hooks: J. Robert Oppenheimer watches the Trinity fireball rise over the New Mexico desert at 5:29 AM on July 16, 1945, and recalls the Bhagavad Gita: 'Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds'
A Hispanic soldier from the 200th Coast Artillery marches the Bataan Death March, sustained only by the memory of his village in the Sangre de Cristos
A Navajo Code Talker transmits the word for 'turtle' — code for 'tank' — on Iwo Jima, using a language the U.S. government once tried to destroy
An hispano rancher on the Pajarito Plateau is told he must leave his land — the government needs it for a purpose no one will explain

key_events: Los Alamos and Sandia National Laboratories expand as Cold War nuclear weapons centers
Uranium mining boom on the Navajo Nation and in the Grants area
White Sands Missile Range tests rockets and missiles
Route 66 tourism peaks before the interstate era
Roswell 'incident' enters popular culture (the 1947 event gains renewed attention)
New Mexico's population grows significantly with defense-related immigration
1950s.economic_conditions: The Cold War made New Mexico a center of nuclear weapons research, production, and testing. Los Alamos, Sandia National Laboratories, Kirtland Air Force Base, and White Sands together made the state one of the most defense-dependent economies in the nation. Uranium mining in the Grants area and on the Navajo Nation boomed as the Atomic Energy Commission bought ore for weapons. Tourism continued growing. The traditional pastoral economy of the hispano villages and Navajo Nation seemed increasingly disconnected from the high-tech military economy.
1950s.migration_patterns: Scientists, engineers, and military personnel continued arriving for defense-related work. Albuquerque grew rapidly. Los Alamos became a prosperous scientific community. Uranium mining brought workers to the Grants area and Navajo Nation. Hispanic and Pueblo communities maintained their traditional settlements but young people increasingly left for urban jobs.
1950s.social_context: New Mexico embodied Cold War contradictions. At Los Alamos, some of the world's finest minds built weapons of unprecedented destruction. Navajo and Hispanic uranium miners extracted radioactive ore with minimal safety protections, unaware of the long-term health consequences. The state's three cultures — Anglo, hispano, and indigenous — occupied increasingly separate economic worlds. Poverty in rural hispano and Pueblo communities persisted alongside nuclear-age prosperity.
1950s.daily_life_snapshot: At Los Alamos, physicists and their families lived in a well-funded community with excellent schools, swimming pools, and cultural events — all dedicated to building nuclear weapons. At the Jackpile mine near Laguna Pueblo, Pueblo and Navajo miners extracted uranium in open pits, breathing radioactive dust. On Route 66 through Albuquerque, neon signs beckoned tourists into motor courts and roadside attractions. In the mountain villages, the acequias still flowed, the church bells still rang.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Uranium mining exposes thousands of workers (especially Navajo and Pueblo) to lethal radiation
Nuclear weapons testing at White Sands and Nevada affects downwind communities
Rural poverty persists in hispano and Pueblo communities despite state-level prosperity
Land and water rights disputes continue affecting indigenous and hispano peoples
narrative_hooks: A Navajo uranium miner at the Kerr-McGee mine descends into the shaft without a respirator, breathing dust that will kill him in twenty years
A Los Alamos physicist drives past a Pueblo village on his way to work and wonders how the same landscape can contain both ancient adobe and the weapons that could end the world
A tourist on Route 66 stops at a Pueblo trading post and buys a turquoise bracelet, never knowing that the miner who found the stone lives in a house without running water

key_events: Reies Lopez Tijerina leads the Alianza Federal de Mercedes to reclaim hispano land grants
Courthouse Raid at Tierra Amarilla (June 5, 1967) — armed hispano activists seize the county courthouse
Interstate highways replace Route 66 — New Mexico's roadside culture changes
Nuclear weapons research continues at Los Alamos and Sandia
Pueblo and Navajo nations continue legal battles for land, water, and sovereignty
Taos Pueblo fights for return of Blue Lake (sacred site) — achieved in 1970
1960s.economic_conditions: The 1960s saw New Mexico's economy increasingly bifurcated — the high-tech military-scientific sector centered on Albuquerque and Los Alamos, and the impoverished rural sector of hispano villages, Pueblo communities, and the Navajo Nation. Federal defense spending remained the dominant economic force. Tourism grew. The interstate highway system changed travel patterns but bypassed many Route 66 communities. Agriculture and ranching continued to decline in relative importance.
1960s.migration_patterns: Urban growth concentrated in Albuquerque, which passed 200,000 residents. Los Alamos and Santa Fe grew. Rural depopulation continued as young people left villages for urban employment. Hispanic civil rights activism — particularly Tijerina's movement — tried to reverse the land grant losses that had impoverished hispano communities. Navajo and Pueblo communities maintained their reservations while fighting for water rights and sovereignty.
1960s.social_context: Reies Lopez Tijerina's Alianza movement was the most dramatic expression of hispano land grant grievances since Las Gorras Blancas. The 1967 Tierra Amarilla courthouse raid — in which armed Alianza members seized the Rio Arriba County courthouse — shocked the state and drew national attention to hispano land claims. The civil rights era empowered indigenous communities as well — Taos Pueblo's fight for Blue Lake became a landmark sovereignty case. New Mexico's three cultures were all asserting their rights simultaneously.
1960s.daily_life_snapshot: At Tierra Amarilla, armed hispano activists stormed the county courthouse on June 5, 1967, demanding justice for centuries of land theft — the National Guard was called in, and Tijerina became a national figure. In Albuquerque, the city sprawled along the Rio Grande, its economy tied to Kirtland Air Force Base and Sandia Labs. At Taos Pueblo, elders made their case for the return of Blue Lake to anyone who would listen. In the mountain villages, the old ways persisted — acequias, fiestas, santos — but the young people were leaving.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Tierra Amarilla courthouse raid (1967) — armed confrontation highlights hispano land grant injustice
Uranium mining legacy: cancer rates rise among former miners and their families
Rural poverty persists despite state economic growth
Route 66 communities decline as interstate highways bypass them
narrative_hooks: Reies Lopez Tijerina stands in the Rio Arriba County courthouse with a rifle, demanding the return of lands stolen from hispano communities over a century — a revolution that began with a Spanish land grant in 1750

A Taos Pueblo elder testifies before Congress about Blue Lake, the sacred site that the U.S. government took for a national forest — he speaks of water, prayer, and a connection to the land that no law can sever
A Los Alamos physicist watches the Tierra Amarilla confrontation on television and realizes that the poverty visible from his laboratory windows has a history stretching back to the conquest
A Navajo woman whose father died from uranium mining-related cancer files a claim with the government — the same government that bought the ore that killed him

An hispano grandmother in a mountain village ladles posole at a community feast, maintaining traditions that have survived four centuries of conquest, revolt, and change — and will survive whatever comes next
